---
title: "Presentation Title"
author: "Author"
format: 
  revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    logo: img/logo.png
    footer: "Organization"
    incremental: true
    theme: [simple]
---
![](img/hardFit_0.png)

# Why fitting is hard and what to do about it

__Seth Blumberg\, MD PhD__

__University of Washington__

_Contributors:_

__Bobby Reiner\, PhD__

__University of Washington__

__Jonathan __  __Dushoff__  __\, PhD__

__McMaster University__


---


![](img/hardFit_1.png)

__Seth Blumberg\, MD PhD__

__University of Washington__

_Contributors:_

__Bobby Reiner\, PhD__

__University of Washington__

__Jonathan __  __Dushoff__  __\, PhD__

__McMaster University__


---


__Thinking about data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Data wrangling__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 9__ </span>

__Foundations of dynamic models and model structure:__

__Variability\, sampling distributions & simulation __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to infectious disease data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to statistical philosophy__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__†__  __ __  _Dynamical fever and model worlds _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Public health\, epidemiology\, and models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to likelihood__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__Statistics and Data Science__

__Introduction to dynamic modelling of infectious disease  I & II __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__‡__  __ Formulating research questions __  _ _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__‡ __  __Creating model worlds__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__‡ __  __Model description__  __& assumptions__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 5__ </span>

__Likelihood and model fitting__

__i\.e\. Integration of data and models__

__Life cycle of a __  __modelling project __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __8__ </span>

__Hidden assumptions of simple ODE models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Modelling for policy and in practice__

__Modeling for policy __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __11__ </span>

__Fitting dynamic models __  __I & II  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5 & 8__ </span>  __ __

__† __  _HIV in Harare _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

_Non exponential waiting times_

__Breaking assumptions\!__

__Mentor presentations __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5__ </span>

__Consequences of heterogeneity and __  __modelling options__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__Introduction to MCMC __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __9__ </span>

__† __  _Application\-based tutorials_

__Guest lectures  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2 &10 __ </span>

_† _  _Consequences of heterogeneity _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__Model assessment__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ __ </span>  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to stochastic models__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>  __Discrete individuals__

__‡__  __ __  __Poster presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 1 & 2__ </span>

__ __  __‡ __  __Collaborative group projects__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 6\-12__ </span>  __ __  __with presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 12__ </span>


---


![](img/hardFit_2.png)

# Clostridioides difficile

![](img/hardFit_3.png)

![](img/hardFit_4.jpg)

<span style="color:#000000">https://</span>  <span style="color:#000000">www\.nature\.com</span>  <span style="color:#000000">/articles/nrmicro2164</span>

<span style="color:#000000">https://</span>  <span style="color:#000000">link\.springer\.com</span>  <span style="color:#000000">/article/10\.1007/s40121\-016\-0140\-z</span>


---


![](img/hardFit_5.png)


---


__Why would I get the wrong answer?__



* Observation error
  * Observational noise
  * Data could be biased
* Process error
  * Dynamical noise
* Model misspecification
  * Population structure may be wrong
  * Model dynamics may be wrong
* Multi\-parameter estimation
  * There may be different parameter combinations that work equally well
  * Parameters may not be “identifiable”


__Multiple errors at once is the norm\!__


---


__Motivating Example – observation error__

![](img/hardFit_6.png)


---


__Observational noise__

![](img/hardFit_7.png)

![](img/hardFit_8.png)

__Distribution of new cases on day 7__

![](img/hardFit_9.png)


---


__Latent variables__



* We model the  _observed_  variables
  * E\.g\.\, reported cases\, estimated prevalence
* Using  _unobserved_  latent variables
  * True cases\, true prevalence\, true number of susceptibles
* Requires a framework that can address the fact that our latent variables have many possible values for each observed variable

---


# Choosing a distribution for observation error

__Finite __

__population__

Case counts are  _individuals_

Poisson and binomial distributions do not reflect individual\-level variation


---


# The world is complicated

![](img/hardFit_10.jpg)

![](img/hardFit_11.jpg)

![](img/hardFit_12.jpg)

![](img/hardFit_13.jpg)

::: {.notes}

Networks can cause an amplification effect so that secondary transmission is stronger

:::


---


# Choosing a distribution for observation error

__Finite __

__population__

__Population\-level __

__variation__

Negative binomial


---


__Observational noise__

![](img/hardFit_14.png)

![](img/hardFit_15.png)

__Distribution of new cases on day 7__

![](img/hardFit_16.png)


---


__Dynamical noise \(process error\)__

![](img/hardFit_17.png)

![](img/hardFit_18.png)


---


__Dynamical noise \(process error\)__

![](img/hardFit_19.png)

![](img/hardFit_20.png)


---


__Thinking about data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Data wrangling__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 9__ </span>

__Foundations of dynamic models and model structure:__

__Variability\, sampling distributions & simulation __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to infectious disease data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to statistical philosophy__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__†__  __ __  _Dynamical fever and model worlds _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Public health\, epidemiology\, and models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to likelihood__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__Statistics and Data Science__

__Introduction to dynamic modelling of infectious disease  I & II __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__‡__  __ Formulating research questions __  _ _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__‡ __  __Creating model worlds__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__‡ __  __Model description__  __& assumptions__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 5__ </span>

__Likelihood and model fitting__

__i\.e\. Integration of data and models__

__Life cycle of a __  __modelling project __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __8__ </span>

__Hidden assumptions of simple ODE models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Modelling for policy and in practice__

__Modeling for policy __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __11__ </span>

__Fitting dynamic models __  __I & II  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5 & 8__ </span>  __ __

__† __  _HIV in Harare _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

_Non exponential waiting times_

__Breaking assumptions\!__

__Mentor presentations __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5__ </span>

__Consequences of heterogeneity and __  __modelling options__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__Introduction to MCMC __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __9__ </span>

__† __  _Application\-based tutorials_

__Guest lectures  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2 &10 __ </span>

_† _  _Consequences of heterogeneity _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__Model assessment__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ __ </span>  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to stochastic models__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>  __Discrete individuals__

__‡__  __ __  __Poster presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 1 & 2__ </span>

__ __  __‡ __  __Collaborative group projects__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 6\-12__ </span>  __ __  __with presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 12__ </span>


---


__Likelihood approaches \(technicalities\)__


---


__Observational and dynamical noise__

![](img/hardFit_21.png)


---


__Observational and dynamical noise__

![](img/hardFit_22.png)

__Dealing with observation and process error at the same time is hard\!__


---


__Modern Computational Approaches__

There is an ever\-increasing collection of advanced tools for model fitting\. A \(very\) partial list:



  * __MCMC__
  * __POMP – Partially observed Markov processes__
  * __JAGS / __  __WinBUGS__  __ / BUGS / __  __OpenBUGS__
  * __Stan__
  * __BayesianTools__
  * __Greta__
  * __pymc3__
  * __INLA – integrated nested Laplacian approximation__
  * __TMB – template model builder__
  * __ODIN/DUST__
  * __Macpan__
  * __Julia libraries \(Turing\, JL\)__

---


__Thinking about data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Data wrangling__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 9__ </span>

__Foundations of dynamic models and model structure:__

__Variability\, sampling distributions & simulation __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to infectious disease data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to statistical philosophy__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__†__  __ __  _Dynamical fever and model worlds _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Public health\, epidemiology\, and models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to likelihood__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__Statistics and Data Science__

__Introduction to dynamic modelling of infectious disease  I & II __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__‡__  __ Formulating research questions __  _ _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__‡ __  __Creating model worlds__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__‡ __  __Model description__  __& assumptions__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 5__ </span>

__Likelihood and model fitting__

__i\.e\. Integration of data and models__

__Life cycle of a __  __modelling project __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __8__ </span>

__Hidden assumptions of simple ODE models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Modelling for policy and in practice__

__Modeling for policy __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __11__ </span>

__Fitting dynamic models __  __I & II  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5 & 8__ </span>  __ __

__† __  _HIV in Harare _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

_Non exponential waiting times_

__Breaking assumptions\!__

__Mentor presentations __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5__ </span>

__Consequences of heterogeneity and __  __modelling options__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__Introduction to MCMC __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __9__ </span>

__† __  _Application\-based tutorials_

__Guest lectures  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2 &10 __ </span>

_† _  _Consequences of heterogeneity _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__Model assessment__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ __ </span>  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to stochastic models__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>  __Discrete individuals__

__‡__  __ __  __Poster presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 1 & 2__ </span>

__ __  __‡ __  __Collaborative group projects__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 6\-12__ </span>  __ __  __with presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 12__ </span>


---


__Model misspecification__

![](img/hardFit_23.png)

![](img/hardFit_24.png)


---


__Thinking about data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Data wrangling__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 9__ </span>

__Foundations of dynamic models and model structure:__

__Variability\, sampling distributions & simulation __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to infectious disease data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to statistical philosophy__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__†__  __ __  _Dynamical fever and model worlds _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Public health\, epidemiology\, and models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to likelihood__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__Statistics and Data Science__

__Introduction to dynamic modelling of infectious disease  I & II __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__‡__  __ Formulating research questions __  _ _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__‡ __  __Creating model worlds__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__‡ __  __Model description__  __& assumptions__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 5__ </span>

__Likelihood and model fitting__

__i\.e\. Integration of data and models__

__Life cycle of a __  __modelling project __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __8__ </span>

__Hidden assumptions of simple ODE models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Modelling for policy and in practice__

__Modeling for policy __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __11__ </span>

__Fitting dynamic models __  __I & II  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5 & 8__ </span>  __ __

__† __  _HIV in Harare _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

_Non exponential waiting times_

__Breaking assumptions\!__

__Mentor presentations __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5__ </span>

__Consequences of heterogeneity and __  __modelling options__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__Introduction to MCMC __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __9__ </span>

__† __  _Application\-based tutorials_

__Guest lectures  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2 &10 __ </span>

_† _  _Consequences of heterogeneity _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__Model assessment__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ __ </span>  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to stochastic models__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>  __Discrete individuals__

__‡__  __ __  __Poster presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 1 & 2__ </span>

__ __  __‡ __  __Collaborative group projects__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 6\-12__ </span>  __ __  __with presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 12__ </span>


---


__Multi\-parameter inference__

Modern methods are already hard\, and when you try to fit more than one parameter at once\, you add considerable complexity

Once you do multi\-parameter inference\, you may find that confidence intervals are very large – this may reflect the reality of knowledge\, but may not make you look good

Parameter identifiability can be challenging\.

![](img/hardFit_25.png)

::: {.notes}

Unmeasured heterogeneity may lead to the conclusion that observed variation in outcomes was due to stochasticity

:::


---


A good fit is not necessarily a useful fit

![](img/hardFit_26.png)

Many tools for addressing overfitting including:

Akaike Information Criterion \(AIC\)

Bayesian Information Criterion

Elastic Net

Cross\-validation

::: {.notes}

Unmeasured heterogeneity may lead to the conclusion that observed variation in outcomes was due to stochasticity

:::


---


__Personal philosophy: __ Don’t blame the data\!

![](img/hardFit_27.jpg)



  * See incomplete data as a modeling opportunity
  * If there is enough data to tell you an answer with clarity\, you don’t need a model\!
  * If data is insufficient\, modeling can help provide insight into efficient data collection
::: {.notes}

Unmeasured heterogeneity may lead to the conclusion that observed variation in outcomes was due to stochasticity

:::


---


Making your fit into a likelihood clarifies assumptions and creates a foundation for statistical inference

Accounting for both process and observation error is harder\, but there are a wealth of modern\, computational techniques to facilitate this

Multi\-parameter inference can also be challenging\, particularly when data is limited\.  \(But Carl will talk about Markov Chain Monte Carlo techniques for this tomorrow\)


---


![](img/hardFit_28.jpg)


---


![](img/hardFit_29.png)

# Why fitting is hard and what to do about it



* <span style="color:#000000"> _C\. difficile_ </span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Mpox trend</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error</span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Trachoma elimination</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error\, process error\, </span> misspecification – Oh my\!
* <span style="color:#000000">Measles emergence</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Multi\-parameter estimation</span>

---


![](img/hardFit_30.png)



* <span style="color:#7030a0"> _C\. difficile_ </span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Mpox trend</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error</span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Trachoma elimination</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error\, process error\, </span> misspecification – Oh my\!
* <span style="color:#000000">Measles emergence</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Multi\-parameter estimation</span>

---


![](img/hardFit_31.png)


---


# Reproduction number vs amplification

![](img/hardFit_32.png)

![](img/hardFit_33.png)

Sometimes no formal adjustments of process / observation error or misspecification are needed\!


---


![](img/hardFit_34.png)

# Why fitting is hard and what to do about it



* <span style="color:#000000"> _C\. difficile_ </span>
* <span style="color:#7030a0"> __Mpox trend__ </span>
  * <span style="color:#7030a0"> __Observation error__ </span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Trachoma elimination</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error\, process error\, </span> misspecification – Oh my\!
* <span style="color:#000000">Measles emergence</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Multi\-parameter estimation</span>

---


![](img/hardFit_35.png)

![](img/hardFit_36.png)

__Stuttering chains \(__  _R_  __0__  __<1\)__

__Epidemics and persistence__

__\(__  _R_  __0 __  __> 1\)__

__Circulation in reservoir__

__Cross\-species spillover__

![](img/hardFit_37.png)

Active

Surveillance

Rimoin et al\. PNAS 2010


---


![](img/hardFit_38.png)

__Outbreak size is a marker of human transmissibility__


---


![](img/hardFit_40.png)

__Outbreak size is a marker of human transmissibility__

| 1980\-1984 | 2005\-2007 |
| :-: | :-: |
| Experienced\, well\-trained field teams | Relatively Inexperienced field team |
| Well Funded | Limited Resources |
| Monetary rewards for case identification | No financial rewards |
| Retrospective case identification by contact tracing and serology | Case identification required sampling during active infection |

![](img/hardFit_42.png)


---


![](img/hardFit_43.png)

__Case detection Bias__

Observed Infection

__Perfect detection  \(Unrealistic\)__

Detection Probability = 100%

Observed mean outbreak size = 3\.5

![](img/hardFit_44.gif)


---


![](img/hardFit_45.png)

__Case detection Bias__

Retrospectively identified

Infection

Observed Infection

Unobserved Infection

__Perfect detection  \(Unrealistic\)__

Detection Probability = 100%

Observed mean outbreak size = 3\.5

![](img/hardFit_46.gif)

__Size\-dependent \(1980\-86\)__

Detection Probability = 72%

Observed mean outbreak size = 5

![](img/hardFit_47.gif)


---


![](img/hardFit_48.png)

__Case detection Bias__

Retrospectively identified

Infection

Observed Infection

Unobserved Infection

__Perfect detection  \(Unrealistic\)__

Detection Probability = 100%

Observed mean outbreak size = 3\.5

![](img/hardFit_49.gif)

__Size\-dependent \(1980\-86\)__

Detection Probability = 72%

Observed mean outbreak size = 5

![](img/hardFit_50.gif)

__Random \(2005\-07\)__

Detection Probability = 43%

Observed mean outbreak size = 3

![](img/hardFit_51.gif)


---


![](img/hardFit_52.png)



* <span style="color:#000000"> _C\. difficile_ </span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Mpox trend</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error</span>
* <span style="color:#7030a0"> __Trachoma elimination__ </span>
  * <span style="color:#7030a0"> __Observation error\, process error\, __ </span>  <span style="color:#7030a0"> __misspecification – Oh my\!__ </span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Measles emergence</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Multi\-parameter estimation</span>

---


![](img/hardFit_53.png)

https://www\.trachomaatlas\.org/


---


![](img/hardFit_54.jpg)


---


Global progress towards trachoma control:

![](img/hardFit_55.jpg)

But\, we’re not there yet …


---


# Data overview

|  | __All data__ |
| :-: | :-: |
| __Years__ | __2004 \- 2023__ |
| __Surveys / TF data points \(n\)__ | __7513__ |
| __Trachomatous inflammation – Follicular __ |  |
| Mean TF Prevalence % \(range\) | __9\.__  __1__  __ \(0 \- 80\.1\)__ |
| Median TF Prevalence % \(IQR\) | __4\.__  __1__  __ \(1\.2 \- 11\.__  __7__  __\)__ |
| __Trachomatous Trichiasis __ |  |
| __Mean TT __ Prevalence %  __\(range\)__ | __0\.9 \(0 \- 23\.5\)__ |
| Median TT Prevalence % \(IQR\) | __0\.3 \(0\.1 – 1\.0\)__ |
| __Countries__ | __56__ |
| __IUs represented__ | __3717__ |
| __Rounds of MDA – mean \(range\)__ | __2\.3 \(0 \- 16\)__ |

::: {.notes}

Try diff kernel or explain the bump near zero (not at 0)

:::


---


# Overview of methods

Candidate models developed

<span style="color:#0b5394"> __As new data becomes available__ </span>

Models trained on training dataset

\(2004 \- 2018\)

<span style="color:#0b5394">Models re\-trained with updated data</span>

Models scored & weights derived based on the scoring dataset \(2019 \- 2021\)

<span style="color:#0b5394">Models re\-scores & updated weights derived</span>

Models and ensemble retrained on training and scoring datasets \(2004 \- 2021\)

Ensemble performance assessedbased on evaluation dataset \(2022 \- 2023\)


---


# Model weights (2019-21)

![](img/hardFit_56.png)

![](img/hardFit_57.png)


---


![](img/hardFit_58.png)

<span style="color:#7030a0">Feasibility assessment for achieving WHO 2030 goals for global elimination as a public health problem\.</span>

<span style="color:#7030a0">District\-specific treatment and surveillance recommendations – even in districts where recent surveillance is not available</span>

<span style="color:#7030a0">Impact evaluation of specific disease control programs \(e\.g\.\, Accelerate\)</span>

<span style="color:#7030a0">Scientific insight into importance of geographic\, programmatic\, and environmental features</span>

<span style="color:#7030a0">Insight into  whether apparent disease re\-emergence is true re\-emergence or noise artifact</span>

::: {.notes}

Amhara and Oromia

:::


---


![](img/hardFit_59.png)

# Why fitting is hard and what to do about it



* <span style="color:#000000"> _C\. difficile_ </span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Mpox trend</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error</span>
* <span style="color:#000000">Trachoma elimination</span>
  * <span style="color:#000000">Observation error\, process error\, </span> misspecification – Oh my\!
* <span style="color:#7030a0"> __Measles emergence__ </span>
  * <span style="color:#7030a0"> __Multi\-parameter estimation__ </span>

---


# Chain size data for measles in the U.S.

|  | Isolated cases | 2 cases | 3\-5 cases | >5 cases |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 2002 | 21 | 2 | 2 | 1 |
| 2003 | 23 | 3 | 1 | 2 |
| 2004 | 14 | 5 | 1 | 1 |
| 2005 | 18 | 4 | 2 | 1 |
| 2006 | 26 | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| 2007 | 21 | 2 | 3 | 1 |
| 2008 | 27 | 3 | 3 | 6 |
| 2009 | 17 | 3 | 4 | 4 |
| 2010 | 37 | 6 | 4 | 0 |
| 2011 | 82 | 12 | 8 | 8 |


---


__Number of chains__

# Inference of transmission parameters

Branching

process

theory

Maximum likelihood estimation provides:

Effective reproductive number

Average chain size

Dispersion parameter

Probability of an outbreak \(> 3 cases\)


---


# Chain size distribution

![](img/hardFit_70.png)

__More __

__superspreading__

![](img/hardFit_71.png)

<span style="color:#17375e"> __Homogeneous__ </span>

<span style="color:#17375e"> __transmission__ </span>

The use of a negative binomial distribution to describe the transmission potential of an individual case allows variability in both the strength and heterogeneity of transmission\.


---


# Measles USA (2002-2011)

![](img/hardFit_72.png)

Homogenous

spread

<span style="color:#7030a0">Superspreading</span>

<span style="color:#7030a0">capacity</span>

<span style="color:#17375e">To match the data\, models must incorporate both the</span>

<span style="color:#17375e">strength AND the heterogeneity of transmission</span>


---


![](img/hardFit_73.png)

<span style="color:#17375e">To match the data\, models must incorporate both the</span>

<span style="color:#17375e">strength AND the heterogeneity of transmission</span>


---


# Stable vaccine coverage is crucial!

![](img/hardFit_74.png)


---


|  | __Benefit__ | __Weakness__ |
| :-: | :-: | :-: |
| __Purpose__ | Good for selecting models with better predictive performance | Not intended to identify the “true” model |
| __Model Fit__ | Penalizes complexity to avoid overfitting | May still favor overly complex models\, especially in large samples |
| __Flexibility__ | Can be used across a wide range of models | Assumes likelihood is correctly specified |
| __Priors__ | Frequentist approach—no prior assumptions required | Doesn’t incorporate prior knowledge \(unlike Bayesian approaches\) |

![](img/hardFit_75.png)

::: {.notes}

Unmeasured heterogeneity may lead to the conclusion that observed variation in outcomes was due to stochasticity

:::


---


# United States vs Canada


---


__Thinking about data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Data wrangling__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 9__ </span>

__Foundations of dynamic models and model structure:__

__Variability\, sampling distributions & simulation __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to infectious disease data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to statistical philosophy__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__†__  __ __  _Dynamical fever and model worlds _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Public health\, epidemiology\, and models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to likelihood__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__Statistics and Data Science__

__Introduction to dynamic modelling of infectious disease  I & II __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__‡__  __ Formulating research questions __  _ _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__‡ __  __Creating model worlds__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__‡ __  __Model description__  __& assumptions__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 5__ </span>

__Likelihood and model fitting__

__i\.e\. Integration of data and models__

__Life cycle of a __  __modelling project __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __8__ </span>

__Hidden assumptions of simple ODE models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Modelling for policy and in practice__

__Modeling for policy __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __11__ </span>

__Fitting dynamic models __  __I & II  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5 & 8__ </span>  __ __

__† __  _HIV in Harare _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

_Non exponential waiting times_

__Breaking assumptions\!__

__Mentor presentations __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5__ </span>

__Consequences of heterogeneity and __  __modelling options__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__Introduction to MCMC __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __9__ </span>

__† __  _Application\-based tutorials_

__Guest lectures  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2 &10 __ </span>

_† _  _Consequences of heterogeneity _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__Model assessment__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ __ </span>  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to stochastic models__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>  __Discrete individuals__

__‡__  __ __  __Poster presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 1 & 2__ </span>

__ __  __‡ __  __Collaborative group projects__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 6\-12__ </span>  __ __  __with presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 12__ </span>


---


__Thinking about data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Data wrangling__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 9__ </span>

__Foundations of dynamic models and model structure:__

__Variability\, sampling distributions & simulation __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to infectious disease data __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to statistical philosophy__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__†__  __ __  _Dynamical fever and model worlds _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Public health\, epidemiology\, and models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__Introduction to likelihood__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__Statistics and Data Science__

__Introduction to dynamic modelling of infectious disease  I & II __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __1__ </span>

__‡__  __ Formulating research questions __  _ _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__‡ __  __Creating model worlds__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 4__ </span>

__‡ __  __Model description__  __& assumptions__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 5__ </span>

__Likelihood and model fitting__

__i\.e\. Integration of data and models__

__Life cycle of a __  __modelling project __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __8__ </span>

__Hidden assumptions of simple ODE models __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2__ </span>

__Modelling for policy and in practice__

__Modeling for policy __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __11__ </span>

__Fitting dynamic models __  __I & II  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5 & 8__ </span>  __ __

__† __  _HIV in Harare _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

_Non exponential waiting times_

__Breaking assumptions\!__

__Mentor presentations __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __5__ </span>

__Consequences of heterogeneity and __  __modelling options__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>

__Introduction to MCMC __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __9__ </span>

__† __  _Application\-based tutorials_

__Guest lectures  __  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __2 &10 __ </span>

_† _  _Consequences of heterogeneity _  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __3__ </span>

__Model assessment__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ __ </span>  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __10__ </span>

__Introduction to stochastic models__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 3__ </span>  __Discrete individuals__

__‡__  __ __  __Poster presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 1 & 2__ </span>

__ __  __‡ __  __Collaborative group projects__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 6\-12__ </span>  __ __  __with presentations__  <span style="color:#ffffff"> __ 12__ </span>


---




* <span style="color:#002060">Meaningful fitting requires:</span>
  * <span style="color:#7030a0">Contextualized understanding of the  research question and impact of modeling results</span>
  * <span style="color:#0070c0">Quantitative understanding of data sources\, limitations and biases</span>
  * <span style="color:#00a7b0">Humble consideration of model assumptions and weaknesses</span>
  * <span style="color:#00b050">Patience\!</span>

---


__Make\-up sessions__

<span style="color:#7030a0"> __Tonight 7:00 – 8:00 PM__ </span>

<span style="color:#7030a0"> __Tomorrow 7:00 \- 7:45 AM__ </span>

<span style="color:#7030a0"> __Tonight 7:00 – 8:00 PM__ </span>

<span style="color:#7030a0"> __Wednesday 7:00 – 8:00 PM__ </span>

![](img/hardFit_77.jpg)

![](img/hardFit_78.png)

![](img/hardFit_79.jpg)


---


![](img/hardFit_80.png)

![](img/hardFit_81.png)

__This presentation is made available through a Creative Commons Attribution\-Noncommercial license\. Details  of the license and permitted uses are available at__

<span style="color:#0563c1"> _[http://creativecommons\.org/licenses/by/3\.0/](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/)_ </span>

__© __  _2014\-2023 _  __International Clinics on Infectious Disease Dynamics and Data__

<span style="color:#0563c1"> _Thumbi_ </span>  <span style="color:#0563c1"> _ SM\, Bruce F\, Bellan SE\, Pulliam JRCP\. “Introduction to Infectious Disease Data” Clinic on the Meaningful Modeling of _ </span>  <span style="color:#0563c1"> </span>  <span style="color:#0563c1"> _Epidemiological Data\. https://_ </span>  <span style="color:#0563c1"> _doi\.org_ </span>  <span style="color:#0563c1"> _/10\.6084/m9\.figshare\.5044603\.v4_ </span>

__Attribution:__

__Thumbi__  __ SM\, Bruce F\, Bellan SE\, Pulliam JRCP__

__Clinic on the Meaningful Modeling of Epidemiological Data__

__Source URL:__

<span style="color:#0563c1"> _https://_ </span>  <span style="color:#0563c1"> _doi\.org_ </span>  <span style="color:#0563c1"> _/10\.6084/m9\.figshare\.5044603\.v4_ </span>

__For further information please contact __  <span style="color:#0563c1"> _[figshare@ici3d\.org](mailto:figshare@ici3d.org)_ </span>  _[\.](mailto:figshare@ici3d.org)_

![](img/hardFit_82.png)

![](img/hardFit_83.png)

![](img/hardFit_84.png)


---


__Robustness of conclusions__

Why do we fit models to data in infectious disease epidemiology?

In most contexts\, the final goal is to evaluate options for reducing disease to improve public health\.

We know every model is wrong\. The question here is: “are the simplifying assumptions of our model leading us to the wrong conclusion”?

Specifically\, consider conclusions that focus on intervention comparisons and optimal intervention design\.

::: {.notes}

[ASK STUDENTS: WHAT ARE SOME UNREALISTIC ASSUMPTIONS OF SIMPLE MODELS THAT WE’VE DISCUSSED THIS WEEK?]


:::


---


__Robustness of conclusions__

How to assess “robustness” of your conclusions:

Try to parameterize your model with different data sets\. Do the eventual intervention recommendations change?

Try to add more complexity \(that you think is important\) to your model and re\-fit\. Do the eventual intervention recommendations change?

_Validate the inference\, _  _not the model or method you’re working with_


---




* We need  __dynamics__  to understand links between processes and outcomes
  * How do things work?
* We need  __statistics__  to understand uncertainty
  * What can we learn from  _data_

---


When deciding on the complexity of our model world\, we must consider  __what__  we will use our model for\.

If we made different choices\, would resulting policy recommendations change drastically?

If we ignore key features of reality\, our model may fit our data well\, but misrepresent the relative and absolute impact of various interventions\.

::: {.notes}

2) through time or between individuals

:::
